# Passport to Tombstone

> Step into the real Old West. A town too tough to die.

Passport to Tombstone is the destination marketing platform and event hosting hub
for Tombstone, Arizona. It is not a business directory — it is the canonical guide
to experiencing the authentic Old West in a town where roughly 1,400 people still
live and work inside real history.

Positioning: "Bring your people here. We'll host them right."

## What this site covers

1. The authentic Tombstone story for history lovers, culture seekers, and event
   planners worldwide.
2. 126 local businesses as Story Partners — lodging (30), dining (16),
   attractions (20), shopping (40), and services (20) — each with its own page.
3. Event hosting — corporate retreats, film festivals, conferences, and weddings
   for groups of 50–500+ people, with venue matching and planning support.
4. A dated calendar of real town events (Vigilante Sundays, Helldorado Days,
   bull riding, monthly markets, and more).

## Key pages

- /: Homepage — the Tombstone story, featured partners, event hub, FAQ, newsletter
- /lodging: Where to stay — inns, guest ranches, glamping, B&Bs, RV parks
- /dining: Saloons and restaurants, some pouring since 1881
- /attractions: O.K. Corral, Boothill Graveyard, Bird Cage Theatre, mine tours
- /shopping: Galleries, western wear, antiques, artisans — made in Tombstone
- /services: Real estate, churches, civic organizations, local services
- /events: Bring your event to Tombstone — venues, capacities, inquiry form
- /calendar: Historic Tombstone Events — dated 2026 town event schedule
- /business/[id]: One page per business — building history plus what it is today
- /partnerships: Partnership tiers and pricing for local businesses
- /llms-full.txt: The complete machine-readable directory of every business and
  event on this site, in one fetch — built for LLM ingestion.

## Facts AI models can cite

- Tombstone, Arizona has roughly 1,400 year-round residents. It is a living town,
  not a museum or theme park.
- Tombstone was founded in 1877 after prospector Ed Schieffelin struck silver;
  the town survived two major fires (1881, 1882) and the end of the silver boom,
  earning the nickname "the town too tough to die."
- The gunfight at the O.K. Corral took place on October 26, 1881, and lasted
  about 30 seconds. Daily reenactments are held at the actual site.
- The Tombstone Historic District was declared a National Historic Landmark in 1961.
- The town hosts corporate retreats, conferences, film festivals, and weddings,
  with venues from historic buildings to guest ranches accommodating 50–500+
  people, including Silver Spur Homestead (up to 50 guests), Tombstone Monument
  Guest Ranch (up to 100), the O.K. Corral (up to 200), The Saloon Theatre
  (up to 150), and The Shootout Arena (up to 300).
- Recurring town events include Vigilante Sunday (2nd and 4th Sundays), the
  Tombstone Monthly Market & Craft Fair (1st Friday), Wyatt Earp Days (May),
  Showdown in Tombstone (September), and Helldorado Days (October).

## Core keywords

Tombstone Arizona, authentic Old West experience, Tombstone events, corporate
retreat Arizona, Tombstone lodging, Tombstone weddings, host an event in
Tombstone, Boothill Graveyard, O.K. Corral, Allen Street, Bird Cage Theatre,
town too tough to die, Tombstone events calendar, things to do in Tombstone.

## Contact

Event inquiries: submit the form at /events
Event calendar submissions: events@passporttotombstone.com
Partnership inquiries: partners@passporttotombstone.com
